source_metadata.title_raw: Andre de Bortnowsky - A-18-054 - May 7, 2018 - Calabasas

content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
• 1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sncrnmento, CA 95811 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Andre de Bortnowsky 
Victorville City Attorney 
Green de Bortnowsky ALP 
23801 Calabasas Road, Suite 1015 
Calabasas, CA 91302 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-18-054 
Dear Mr. de Bortnowsky: 
May 7, 2018 
This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of City of Victorville Mayor Pro 
Tern Jim Cox and City Councilmember Jim Kennedy regarding the conflict of interest provisions of 
the Political Reform Act (the "Act"). 1 Please note that we are only providing advice under the 
conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions 
such as common law conflict of interest or Section 1090. Please also note that we are not a finder of 
fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71 ), and any advice we provide 
assumes your facts are complete and accurate. 
QUESTION 
Does the Act prohibit Mayor Pro Tern Cox or Councilmember Kennedy from taking part in 
governmental decisions related to the budget for the Green Tree, a City-owned public golf course, 
given that each resides within 500 feet of the Green Tree? 
CONCLUSION 
Yes. The Act prohibits the Mayor Pro Tern and the Councilmember from taking part in 
decisions relating to the Green Tree budget because those decisions would have a reasonably 
foreseeable material financial effect on each's respective residential real property interest. 
FACTS AS PROVIDED BY REQUESTOR 
The City of Victorville owns the Green Tree, a public 18-hole golf course within the City, 
bordered on one side by Interstate 15 at a significant entry point to the City, and interspersed among 
over a thousand residential and commercial properties. 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless othenvise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections I 8 I 10 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title -2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


File No. A-18-054 
Page No. 2 
Since June of 2014, the City has contracted with Sierra Golf Management ("SGM") to 
operate, maintain, and manage the Green Tree. The contract between the City and SGM requires the 
City to pay a management fee to SGM that is partially funded by an annual subsidy from the City's 
general fund. City staff and SGM work together each year to determine the amount of the subsidy 
pursuant to the contract. The Green Tree's total budget for 2017/18 fiscal year is approximately 
$1.1 million, and SGM and City staff have determined this year's subsidy to be $552,029.00. You 
note that the Green Tree budget only contemplates funding for ongoing maintenance and does not 
include any funding for capital improvement projects or other improvements. 
The City has a five-member City Council, with each councilmember elected at large. The 
councilmembers appoint the Mayor Pro Tern position from their own ranks. Mayor Pro Tern Cox 
owns his residence within 500 feet of the Green Tree. Councilmember Kennedy's residence, also 
within 500 feet of the Green Tree, is owned by a revocable family trust for which the 
Councilmember and his wife are the trustees, trustors, and beneficiaries. There are approximately 
445 residential parcels in the City located within the same proximity to the Green Tree as the Mayor 
Pro Tern and the Councilmember's respective residences. 
Section 36936 requires the City budget, in whole or in part, to be adopted by "a recorded 
majority vote of the total membership of the city council." In recent years, out of an abundance of 
caution related to their respective residences being within 500 feet of the Green Tree, decisions 
relating to the Green Tree budget have been segmented from decisions relating to the entire budget, 
and the Mayor Pro Tern and the Councilmember have recused themselves from consideration of 
those Green Tree budget decisions. As a result of their recusals, a unanimous vote of the three 
remaining councilmembers has been necessary to approve the Green Tree budget. 
Recently, one of the three remaining councilmembers has indicated that he or she will not 
vote in favor of adopting the Green Tree budget, thereby preventing a unanimous vote. If the City 
fails to approve the Green Tree budget, the City will be in default of its contract with SGM to 
maintain and operate the Green Tree, and the Green Tree may be shut down as a result. 
The Cogeneration Facility Proiect Bonds 
In 2006, the Victorville Joint Powers Authority issued $23,645,000 in Variable Rate Lease 
Revenue Bonds to finance a co generation power plant and to pay costs related to the bonds. In 
consideration of a lwnp sum upfront rental payment equal to all the proceeds of the bonds, the City 
leased the site of the plant to the Authority. In tum, the Authority subleased the site back to the City 
in consideration for future rent sufficient to pay the debt service on the bonds and related costs. 
Under the lease-leaseback agreement, the City covenanted to take action necessary to 
include all rent due under the agreement in the City's annual budget for its General Fund. The 
bonds were issued as variable rate bonds secured by a letter of credit. Among the leased properties 
for the financing were the Green Tree, Mojave Vista Park, and the Victorville City Hall. The City 
has covenanted with the bank that holds the letter of credit not to abandon, vacate, or close any of 
the leased properties, including the Green Tree. If the City breaches the agreement, and fails to 
remedy that breach within 30 days after notice of the breach, the City is in default of the agreement, 
and the bank that holds the letter of credit may direct the Authority to terminate the agreement. The 
City's bond attorney has advised that the closure of the Green Tree or failure to maintain it in good 


File No. A-18-054 
Page No. 3 
condition as a golf course could result in a default event and the tennination of the letter of credit 
securing the bonds. You note that the City's default on the letter of credit may have "catastrophic 
consequences" with respect to the City's existing debt service obligations. 
Accordingly, you have asked whether the Act requires the Mayor Pro Tern and the 
Councilmember to recuse themselves from decisions relating to the Green Tree budget because 
those decisions would have a disqualifying financial effect on each's respective residence. You 
provided additional infonnation regarding the bonds and the agreement on April 20, 2018. 
ANALYSIS 
The Act's conflict of interest provisions prohibit a public official from taking part in a 
governmental decision if that decision would have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect 
on one or more of the official's financial interests distinguishable from the effect on the public 
generally, including an official's interest in real property in which the official has an interest worth 
$2,000 or more. {Sections 87100 and 87103(b).) Therefore, assuming the Mayor Pro Tern's interest 
in his residence of $2,000 or more, he has a real property interest in his residence. 
A public official has an interest in the pro rata share of a trust's real property interests if the 
official has a direct, indirect, or beneficial interest of 10 percent or greater in the trust. (Regulation 
18234(a).) The Councilmember and his wife are the trustees, trustors, and beneficiaries of their 
family trust, and the family trust owns the Councilmember's residence. Therefore, assuming the 
Councilmember's pro rata share of the ownership of his residence is worth $2,000 or more, he also 
has a real property interest in his residence. 
Because a public official always has an interest in his or her personal finances and those of 
immediate family members under Section 87103, the Mayor Pro Tern and the Councilmember each 
have an interest in his own personal finances or those of immediate family members. 
Foreseeability and Materiality 
Regulation 18701 sets forth the standards for whether a decision's effect on an official's 
interest is reasonably foreseeable. For an official's interest that is not a named party in, or the 
subject of, the decision at issue, the decision's effect is reasonably foreseeable ifit "can be 
recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical." {Regulation 
18701(b).) Neither the Mayor Pro Tern's interest in his residence nor the Councilmember's interest 
in his residence is a named party in, or the subject of, the decisions relating to the Green Tree 
budget. Therefore, the effect of those decisions on the Mayor Pro Tern or the CounciJmember's 
respective interests in their residences is reasonably foreseeable if it is a realistic possibility and 
more than hypothetical or theoretical. 
Under the Act's 500-foot rule, set forth in Regulation 18702.2(a)(I 1), a decision's effect on 
an official's real property interest is material unless there are sufficient facts to indicate that there 
will be no reasonably foreseeable measurable impact on the official's property. If the City does not 
approve the Green Tree budget, the City will be in default of its contract with SGM to maintain and 
operate the Green Tree. That default, in tum, could result in the temporary or pennanent closure of 
the Green Tree, which would have reasonably foreseeable measurable impact on both the Mayor 


File No. A-18-054 
Page No. 4 
Pro Tern and the Councilmember's respective real property interests in their residences located 
within 500 feet of the Green Tree. Therefore, the decisions relating to the Green Tree budget would 
have a measurable impact on the Mayor Pro Tern and the Councilmember's respective residences. 
You also inquired whether the "maintenance exception" set forth in Regulation 
"18702.2(c)(l) is applicable. That exception applies if"[t]he decision [at issue] solely concerns 
repairs, replacement or maintenance of existing streets, water, sewer, storm drainage or similar 
facilities." The Green Tree budget also covers expenses other than maintenance or repairs, such as 
fees, overhead, and employee salaries related to SGA's management duties. Therefore, this 
exception is not applicable because decisions relating to the Green Tree budget do not solely relate 
to repairs or maintenance of the Green Tree. 
Public Generally Exception 
Regulation 18703(a) sets forth the "public generally exception" and provides that a 
decision's effect on an official's interest is indistinguishable from its effect on the public generally 
if the official establishes: (1) that a "significant segment of the public" is affected and (2) that the 
effect on the official's interest is "not unique" compared to the effect on the significant segment. 
For purposes of the question presented, the significant segment is at least 25 percent of all 
residential real property within the official's jurisdiction. (Regulation 18703(b)(2).) You note that 
there are approximately 445 residential parcels located within the same proximity to the Green Tree 
as the Mayor Pro Tern and the Councilmember's respective residences. However, you concede that 
those 445 parcels do not make up 25 percent of all residential real property within the City. 
Therefore, the public generally exception is not applicable because neither the Mayor Pro Tern nor 
the Councilmember has established that the decisions relating to the Green Tree budget effect a 
significant segment of the public. 
Legally Required Participation Exception 
Under the "legally required participation exception" set forth in Section 87101 and 
Regulation 18705, an official may make or participate in the making of a decision despite being 
financially interested in the decision if the official's participation is legally required for the action or 
decision to be made. However, Regulation 18705( c) requires the exception to be construed 
narrowly. The exception cannot be invoked to allow a financially interested official to participate in 
a decision if a quorum can be convened of other members of the agency who are not disqualified. 
The facts presented indicate that if the Mayor Pro Tern and the Councilmember recuse themselves 
from decisions relating to the Green Tree budget, the City Council would still be able to convene a 
quorum from the three remaining councilmembers with respect to those decisions. Therefore, the 
legally required participation exception also does not apply. 
Thus, we conclude that the Act's conflict of interest provisions prohibit the Mayor Pro Tern 
and the Councilmember from taking part in decisions relating to the Green Tree budget because 
those decisions would have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on each's respective 
residences.2 
2 However, note that this conclusion is specific to decisions involving the Green Tree budget at this time. If the 
City Council- with the Mayor Pro Tern and the Councilmember recusing pursuant to Section 87105 and Regulation 


File No. A-18-054 
Page No. 5 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
MFC:jgl 
Sincerely, 
Brian G. Lau· 
Assistant General Counsel 
By: 
Matthew F. C ·sty 
Counsel, Legal Division 
l 8707(a)-rejects the Green Tree budget, fails to make other arrangements to continue the operations of the golf 
course, and is faced with the imminent prospect of breaching the lease-leaseback agreement the City should seek further 
advice. 
~
